skill.name: claude-reflect
skill.description: Capture Claude Code corrections, review them, and add approved learnings to CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md for future sessions.
skill.summary: Turn reviewed Claude Code corrections into reusable project and global guidance.
skill.author: BayramAnnakov

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical heuristic because the repository combines scripts, shell examples, home-directory file access, URLs, and secret-related terms. Manual review found no evidence of malicious C2, credential exfiltration, or prompt-injection text in the skill files, but confirmed a real elevated-risk design: hooks capture local prompts, /reflect can scan session history, and approved learnings are written to assistant guidance files. Publish with a clear privacy and file-modification warning.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Local Capture of User Prompts
medium_findings.description: The plugin installs hooks that run on UserPromptSubmit, PreCompact, and Bash PostToolUse events. The capture hook stores matching user prompts in a home-directory queue, which is expected behavior but creates a local privacy and data-retention risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The README describes automatic hooks, and the Python capture path directly shows prompt processing and writes to ~/.claude/learnings-queue.json. This is intentional for the skill, but the privacy impact is real.

medium_findings.title: Broad Session History Reading
medium_findings.description: The /reflect workflow can scan Claude Code project session JSONL files, including agent session files, to extract corrections and tool rejections. This is useful for cold starts, but it expands the amount of conversation history processed by the skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command instructions and utility functions explicitly read session files and extract user messages. The feature is user-triggered, so this is elevated but not malicious.

medium_findings.title: Claude CLI Subprocess for Semantic Analysis
medium_findings.description: The semantic detector invokes the Claude CLI with extracted user text to classify reusable learnings. Arguments are constructed as a list, which limits shell injection risk, but the subprocess may send session-derived text to the configured Claude service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are direct and semantically tied to analyzing captured messages. The list-form invocation avoids shell=True, reducing command injection concerns.

medium_findings.title: Writes Approved Learnings to Assistant Configuration Files
medium_findings.description: After user confirmation, /reflect edits global and project CLAUDE.md files and may append a managed section to AGENTS.md. This is the intended purpose, but it can affect future assistant behavior if harmful or overbroad learnings are approved.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow requires final confirmation before writing, but the destination files influence future Claude Code and Codex behavior. This is a clear integrity risk with user mediation.

medium_findings.title: Environment and Secret Names in Tool Error Learning
medium_findings.description: The tool-error extractor matches environment variable names such as DATABASE_URL, API_KEY, SECRET, SUPABASE_KEY, and REDIS_URL in error text. I did not find evidence that it reads .env file contents, but it can surface secret-related names and error snippets in proposed learnings.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The regex patterns clearly match secret-related environment names, while the surrounding code extracts errors from session logs rather than reading environment files directly.

low_findings.title: Documentation and Test Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static detections come from markdown examples, README badges, distribution notes, legacy scripts, or mocked subprocess tests. These findings explain the high static score but do not show malicious network access, C2 behavior, or credential theft.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are badges, marketplace links, and tests that mock subprocess.run. They are static-analysis noise rather than executable malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Instructions with Bash and Home Directory Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The slash commands allow Bash and include shell snippets that read or modify files under ~/.claude. The snippets align with queue processing and target discovery, but they require user trust in the command workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools headers and shell snippets are explicit. The commands are locally scoped, but they access persistent assistant state.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt Injection Exposure in Learning Extraction
dangerous_patterns.description: Captured user messages are placed into a semantic-analysis prompt and may later become proposed memory entries. No embedded prompt-injection attempt was found in the skill files, but untrusted session text can influence proposed CLAUDE.md content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from user text to analysis prompt and proposed learnings is clear. Human review reduces impact, so this is a medium-confidence exposure rather than a confirmed exploit.

content.value_statement: Corrections and preferences from Claude Code sessions are easy to lose after context changes. Claude Reflect captures likely learnings, lets you review them, and writes approved guidance to CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md.
actual_capabilities: Detects correction and preference patterns from Claude Code prompts through hooks.
Stores candidate learnings in a local queue under the Claude configuration directory.
Runs /reflect to review, edit, approve, skip, or deduplicate queued learnings.
Can scan past Claude Code session files for missed corrections when requested.
Writes approved entries to global CLAUDE.md, project CLAUDE.md, and AGENTS.md when present.
Provides commands to view or clear the learning queue without applying changes.
limitations: Requires Claude Code hooks and a local Python runtime to capture prompts automatically.
Pattern detection can capture false positives, so human review is required before applying learnings.
Historical scans process local session history and may expose sensitive conversation details during review.
Semantic validation depends on the Claude CLI and falls back to heuristic detection when unavailable.

use_cases.title: Preserve Team Conventions
use_cases.description: Turn repeated corrections about project structure, commands, or style into reviewed project guidance.
use_cases.target_user: Engineering teams using Claude Code in shared repositories

use_cases.title: Build Personal Coding Preferences
use_cases.description: Save approved global preferences, such as model choices or common tool habits, for future sessions.
use_cases.target_user: Individual developers who use Claude Code daily

use_cases.title: Recover Missed Learnings
use_cases.description: Scan past session history after installation to find useful corrections that were not captured earlier.
use_cases.target_user: Developers setting up Claude Reflect on an existing project

prompt_templates.title: View Pending Learnings
prompt_templates.prompt: Run /view-queue and summarize the pending learnings without applying any changes.

prompt_templates.title: Review Current Queue
prompt_templates.scenario: Standard reflection after a work session
prompt_templates.prompt: Run /reflect and help me review each queued learning before adding anything to CLAUDE.md.

prompt_templates.title: Preview Changes Safely
prompt_templates.scenario: Dry run before editing guidance files
prompt_templates.prompt: Run /reflect --dry-run and show the proposed global, project, and AGENTS.md updates.

prompt_templates.title: Scan and Consolidate History
prompt_templates.prompt: Run /reflect --scan-history --dedupe and propose consolidated learnings from past sessions for my review.

output_examples.input: Run /reflect after I corrected the assistant to use the project test command.
output_examples.output: The skill presents the captured correction, suggests a project-scoped learning, checks for similar entries, and asks for approval before editing files.
The skill shows proposed additions and destinations, marks skipped or duplicate items, and leaves all files unchanged.

output_examples.input: Run /skip-reflect when the queue contains false positives.
output_examples.output: The skill displays the number of queued items, asks for confirmation, and clears the queue only after approval.

best_practices: Review every proposed learning before applying it to global or project guidance.
Use project scope for repository-specific commands, paths, services, and coding conventions.
Run dry runs before large history scans or deduplication changes.
anti_patterns: Do not approve learnings that contain secrets, tokens, passwords, or private customer data.
Do not make one-time task instructions permanent assistant guidance.
Do not run broad history scans when sensitive session data should stay unprocessed.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically edit CLAUDE.md?
faq.answer: The main /reflect workflow requires review and confirmation before applying learnings to guidance files.

faq.question: Where are captured learnings stored?
faq.answer: Candidate learnings are stored locally in the Claude configuration directory as a learning queue.

faq.question: Can I inspect the queue first?
faq.answer: Yes. Use /view-queue to see pending items without processing or writing changes.

faq.question: Can I discard captured items?
faq.answer: Yes. Use /skip-reflect to clear queued learnings after confirmation.

faq.question: Does it work with Codex?
faq.answer: It can write approved learnings to AGENTS.md, which Codex can use when that file exists.

faq.question: What should I review most carefully?
faq.answer: Check historical scan results, secret-related error text, and any learning that changes future assistant behavior.
